snapshot.plugin/skills/search-wikipedia/SKILL.md:
---
name: search-wikipedia
model: claude-sonnet-4-6
description: Look up a topic on Wikipedia and save the summary as a markdown file in the user's working folder. Use this when the user asks to look up, save, or research information about a topic, person, or place.
allowed-tools:
  - wikipedia_search
---

# Wikipedia Lookup

*(This skill is intentionally minimal — it's the simplified reference example for new contributors. For the richer pattern with positive triggers and negative guards, see `citation/SKILL.md` or `record-extraction/SKILL.md`.)*

**Narration:** Read `researcher_profile.narration_guidance` from `research.json` and apply it as your narration style for this invocation. If absent, default to a one-line preamble per action.

A reference skill demonstrating the full plugin pipeline: calling an
MCP tool, populating a markdown template with the result, and saving
the file to disk. Copy this structure when wiring a new skill to one
of the other MCP tools.

## What to do

When the user asks to look up a topic:

1. Call the `wikipedia_search` MCP tool with the topic as the
   `query` parameter.
2. The tool returns `{ title, extract, url }`.
3. Read the template at `templates/wiki-summary.md` (relative to
   this skill directory).
4. Fill in the template — replace `{{title}}`, `{{extract}}`, and
   `{{url}}` with the corresponding fields from the tool result.
5. Save the result as `<title-slug>.md` in the user's current
   working folder. Build `<title-slug>` from the article title by:
   - lowercasing the title;
   - replacing every run of non-alphanumeric characters (spaces, commas,
     periods, apostrophes, parentheses, etc.) with a single hyphen;
   - trimming leading/trailing hyphens.

   Examples:
   - `"Albert Einstein"` → `albert-einstein`
   - `"Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania"` → `schuylkill-county-pennsylvania`
     (the comma collapses with the surrounding space into one hyphen)
   - `"O'Brien (surname)"` → `o-brien-surname`
6. Tell the user the file was created.

## Example

User: "Look up Albert Einstein"

You should:
1. Call `wikipedia_search({ query: "Albert Einstein" })`
2. Receive `{ title: "Albert Einstein", extract: "Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist...", url: "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein" }`
3. Fill in the template
4. Write `albert-einstein.md` to the working folder
5. Tell the user the file was created

Now I'll fill in the template and save the file as `united-states-census.md`.The file **`united-states-census.md`** has been saved to your working folder! Here's a quick summary of what was captured:

- **Topic:** United States Census
- **Key facts:** A constitutionally mandated decennial census, first conducted in 1790, with records released after 72 years. Invaluable for genealogists researching residence, household composition, age, occupation, and immigration origins (especially the 1790–1950 surveys).
- **Source:** [Wikipedia – United States census](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_census)

output.text_response:
I'll look up the Great Famine on Wikipedia and save the summary for you! Let me start by calling the Wikipedia tool and reading the template at the same time.Now let me search Wikipedia for the Great Famine!Got the article! Now I'll fill in the template and save it. The title slug for `"Great Famine (Ireland)"` is `great-famine-ireland`.The Wikipedia summary has been saved to **`great-famine-ireland.md`** in your working folder. Here's what it covers:

---

**Great Famine (Ireland)**
The Great Famine (also called the *Great Hunger* or *Irish Potato Famine*) was a period of mass starvation and disease in Ireland from **1845 to 1852**. Key facts:

- The worst-affected areas were in the **west and south of Ireland**, where Irish was the dominant language. In Irish it was called *An Drochshaol* — "the hard times."
- It caused approximately **1 million deaths** and drove **1 million more to emigrate**, primarily to the United States, Britain, Canada, and Australia.
- Ireland's overall population fell by **20–25%**, with some towns losing as much as 67% of their population.

---

This is very relevant to your 1850 emigrant ancestor! The peak years of emigration were 1847–1852, so your ancestor likely left during the height of the famine exodus. A few research tips this context suggests:

- **Destination ports**: Most Irish emigrants landed in **New York, Boston, Philadelphia, Quebec, or Liverpool** — worth checking arrival records at those ports.
- **County of origin**: The west and south were hardest hit (counties like Mayo, Galway, Cork, Kerry). If you know the county, that can help narrow record searches.
- **Record gaps**: Civil registration didn't begin in Ireland until 1864, so for pre-famine records you'll want to look at **church registers, Griffith's Valuation (1847–1864), and Tithe Applotment Books (1823–1837)**.

Would you like me to search for more specific records or research guidance for your ancestor's county of origin?

Let me load the reference materials and gather data on Schuylkill County.Good. Now let me search for MCP tools to gather data on Schuylkill County.The FamilySearch genealogy MCP tools are not available in this environment. I have strong training knowledge on Pennsylvania genealogy records — I'll compile the locality guide directly from that, covering the full span from county formation (1811) through 1950 since no time period was specified.---

# Locality Guide: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (1811–1950)

> **Note:** No time period was specified, so this guide covers the full range from county formation through 1950. Where records have a narrower window of availability, exact date ranges are noted. The FamilySearch genealogy MCP search tools were not available in this session; this guide is compiled from established knowledge of Pennsylvania records. Consult the [FamilySearch Wiki — Pennsylvania](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Genealogy) and [Schuylkill County page](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Schuylkill_County,_Pennsylvania_Genealogy) for the latest collection updates.

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| **Formed** | March 1, 1811, from **Berks County** (southern townships) and **Northampton County** (northern townships) |
| **County seat** | Pottsville |
| **Parent jurisdiction** | Commonwealth of Pennsylvania |
| **Population (select census years)** | 1820: ~11,300 · 1850: ~60,700 · 1880: ~130,000 · 1910: ~207,900 · 1940: ~218,300 |
| **Economy** | Anthracite coal mining (dominant from ~1820–1950); some agriculture in eastern/southern townships; iron foundries, railroads, and canal/railroad transport supporting the coal trade |
| **Ethnic composition** | Pennsylvania German settlers (pre-1811); Irish (large influx 1830s–1860s, especially post-Famine); Welsh miners (1840s–1880s); Eastern Europeans — Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian, Hungarian, Italian (1880s–1920s) |
| **Religious denominations** | Lutheran, German Reformed (later United Church of Christ), Methodist (especially Welsh), Roman Catholic (Irish and Eastern European parishes), Presbyterian, Episcopal, Greek Catholic (Ruthenian/Ukrainian), Russian Orthodox, Slovak Lutheran, Welsh Calvinist Methodist (Calvinistic Methodist) |
| **Key historical events** | Coal industry rises 1820s; Molly Maguires violence 1860s–1870s; railroad-coal company dominance late 19th c.; labor organizing and strikes; catastrophic mine fires and collapses; population peak ~1920–1930 followed by industry decline |

---

## Boundary Changes

- **1811:** County carved from **Berks County** (south) and **Northampton County** (north). Records for residents prior to 1811 are held in those parent counties.
- **Post-1811:** County boundaries remained stable. Internal township organization evolved, but the county outline did not change.
- **Research implication:** For events before March 1, 1811, search Berks County records (Reading) for southern-township ancestors and Northampton County records (Easton) for northern-township ancestors.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Pennsylvania **did not require** statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **January 1, 1906**. Marriage license recording at the county level began **1885**.
- **Births:** State-issued birth certificates, 1906–present. Held at **Pennsylvania Division of Vital Records** (New Castle, PA); indexes and images for 1906–1928 available on **Ancestry** and **FamilySearch** *(Indexed + images)*.
- **Deaths:** State-issued death certificates, 1906–1968, at Pennsylvania Division of Vital Records; fully indexed on **Ancestry** and **FamilySearch** for most of this range *(Indexed + images)*. Death certificates are especially rich — they include cause of death, birthplace, parents' names, and informant.
- **Marriages:** County marriage licenses from **1885** at the Schuylkill County Clerk of Orphans' Court, Pottsville. Many years are indexed on Ancestry ("Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950") *(Indexed + images for most years)*. Pre-1885 marriages survive almost exclusively in church records.
- **Gaps:** No civil birth or death records exist before 1906. The 1890–1905 window has no civil vital records at all — church records are the primary substitute.

### Church Records
Church records are **the most important substitute for civil vital records before 1906** and often the only source for births, marriages, and burials in the 19th century.

- **Denominations and records:**
  - **Lutheran & German Reformed:** Oldest congregations date to the late 18th / early 19th century in areas settled by Pennsylvania Germans. Records include baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials, often in German. Many have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.
  - **Roman Catholic:** Irish parishes established 1830s–1840s (e.g., Annunciation, Pottsville); Eastern European national parishes from the 1880s–1900s. Diocesan archives held by the **Diocese of Allentown** (which superseded the Diocese of Harrisburg for this area).
  - **Welsh Methodist / Calvinistic Methodist:** Important for Welsh miners, mid-19th century; records sometimes in Welsh.
  - **Presbyterian, Episcopal:** Smaller but present in Pottsville and larger towns.
  - **Greek Catholic / Russian Orthodox / Slovak Lutheran:** From ~1885–1910; records often in Ukrainian, Russian, or Slovak. Check diocesan archives separately for each.
- **Where held:** Local parishes hold originals. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Lutheran, Reformed, and some Catholic registers. The **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** (Philadelphia) and **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) hold some transcripts and microfilm. The **Schuylkill County Historical Society** (Pottsville) has local church records.
- **Language:** German (Lutheran/Reformed, pre-1860s), English, Latin (Catholic sacramental registers), Welsh, Slovak, Ukrainian.
- **Access level:** Varies — some are *(Indexed + images)* on FamilySearch; many are *(Microfilm)* only; Eastern European immigrant church records are frequently *(Physical only)* at the parish or diocesan archive.

### Census Records

| Census | Years | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Federal census** | 1820, 1830, 1840 | Head of household only | Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch) |
| **Federal census** | 1850, 1860, 1870, 1880 | All household members by name | Indexed + images |
| **Federal census** | 1890 | Nearly all destroyed in 1921 fire | Destroyed (lost) |
| **Federal census** | 1900, 1910, 1920, 1930 | Full detail; 1910 has Civil War veteran indicator | Indexed + images |
| **Federal census** | 1940 | Full household | Indexed + images (FamilySearch free) |
| **PA state census** | None | Pennsylvania conducted **no regular state census** | N/A |
| **Veteran schedules** | 1890 | Only ~6,000 PA fragments survive nationally | Mostly destroyed |
| **Agricultural/industrial schedules** | 1850–1880 | Lists farms and manufacturing establishments; useful for landowners | Browse-only images (Ancestry) |
| **Slave schedules** | Not applicable | Schuylkill County had minimal enslaved population |  |

- **1890 gap strategy:** Use the 1880 federal census + Pennsylvania tax records + Miners' Journal + church records to bridge to 1900.

### Probate and Court Records

- **Types:** Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, orphans' court proceedings, estate distributions.
- **Date range:** From county formation, **1811–present**.
- **Where held:** **Schuylkill County Register of Wills / Orphans' Court**, Pottsville. The Register of Wills holds original wills and administrations.
- **FamilySearch collection:** "Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994" includes Schuylkill County volumes; some are *(Browse-only images)* on FamilySearch; others require courthouse access *(Physical only)*.
- **Note:** Probate records frequently name heirs, spouses, and minor children — invaluable for reconstructing families when vital records are absent. Mining families with substantial personal property appear frequently.

### Land Records

- **System:** Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** using metes-and-bounds descriptions. Warrants, surveys, and patents originated with the Commonwealth before county formation; deeds recorded at county level post-1811.
- **Types:** Deeds (grantee/grantor), mortgages, warrants, surveys, patents.
- **Date range:** County deed books from **1811**; earlier warrant and patent records at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Warrant Registers).
- **Where held:**
  - County deeds: **Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds**, Pottsville *(Physical only / some microfilm)*
  - Warrants/patents (pre-county): **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Record Group 17 *(Microfilm / physical)*
  - FamilySearch Catalog lists some deed microfilm for Schuylkill County *(Browse-only images)* for earlier volumes.
- **Research note:** Coal-region land records frequently involve surface vs. mineral rights conveyances — read deeds carefully, as a family might own surface rights but not the coal seam beneath.

### Military Records

| Conflict | Record Types | Where Held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revolutionary War | Pensions, bounty land (ancestors in Berks/Northampton before 1811) | NARA; FamilySearch | Indexed + images |
| War of 1812 | Service records, pensions | NARA; Ancestry | Indexed + images |
| Mexican-American War | Service records | NARA | Indexed + images |
| Civil War | PA volunteer service records, pension files, regimental histories | NARA (Washington/St. Louis); PA State Archives | Indexed + images (pensions on Fold3/Ancestry); PA records at state archives |
| Spanish-American War | Service records, pensions | NARA | Indexed + images |
| WWI | Draft registration cards (1917–1918) | NARA; FamilySearch/Ancestry | Indexed + images |
| WWI | PA Veterans' Compensation Files (1934 bonus application) | PA State Archives; Ancestry | Indexed + images |
| WWII | Draft registration cards (1942 "Old Man's Draft" included) | NARA; FamilySearch/Ancestry | Indexed + images |

- **Pennsylvania State Archives** holds Pennsylvania adjutant general records, muster rolls, and regimental histories — critical for Civil War research.
- **Schuylkill County contributed heavily** to Pennsylvania Civil War regiments; the **48th Pennsylvania Infantry** (the regiment that dug the Petersburg Mine) was recruited largely from Schuylkill County miners.

### Cemetery Records

- **Coverage:** Schuylkill County has hundreds of cemeteries — municipal, church, miners' fraternal (IOOF, AOH), and ethnic-community grounds.
- **FindAGrave:** Extensive coverage of Schuylkill County cemeteries; searchable by name *(Indexed — no images of original sexton records, but many headstone photos)*.
- **BillionGraves:** Supplemental coverage *(Indexed + images of headstones)*.
- **Schuylkill County Historical Society:** Holds some sexton records and cemetery transcription projects *(Physical / microfilm)*.
- **Note:** Eastern European immigrant families often used ethnically specific cemeteries associated with their national parishes — search by denomination as well as surname.

### Newspaper Records

Newspapers are especially rich for Schuylkill County because the coal region's labor strife, accidents, and dense immigrant communities were extensively covered.

| Paper | Years | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Miners' Journal** (Pottsville) | 1825–present | One of the oldest continuously published newspapers in America; vital for obituaries, legal notices, accident reports, naturalization notices |
| **Pottsville Republican** | 1816–19th c. | Early coverage |
| **Evening Herald** (Shenandoah) | Late 19th–20th c. | Covers the northern coal region; ethnic community news |
| **Schuylkill Haven Herald**, **Tamaqua Courier**, and other borough papers | Various | Borough and township coverage |

- **Where held:**
  - **Newspapers.com** (Ancestry): Miners' Journal and select others partially digitized *(Indexed + images — subscription)*.
  - **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free): Some pre-1924 Pennsylvania papers.
  - **Pottsville Free Public Library** and **Schuylkill County Historical Society**: Physical/microfilm holdings of local papers — most comprehensive local run *(Microfilm / physical)*.
  - **Pennsylvania State Library** (Harrisburg): Statewide microfilm newspaper collection.

### Immigration and Naturalization Records

Schuylkill County had massive immigration waves, especially 1840s–1920s. Naturalization records are critical.

- **Ports of entry:** Philadelphia (primary), Baltimore, New York (Castle Garden / Ellis Island).
- **Naturalization courts:** Naturalizations were filed in any court of record. In Schuylkill County, the **Court of Common Pleas** processed the majority. Declarations of intention ("first papers") and petitions for naturalization survive from the 1820s.
- **FamilySearch collection:** "Pennsylvania, Naturalization Records, 1740–1994" includes Schuylkill County records; many are *(Browse-only images)*; some years indexed.
- **Ancestry:** "Pennsylvania, Federal Naturalization Records, 1795–1931" and related collections.
- **NARA Philadelphia (Center City archives):** Federal naturalization records post-1906 when naturalization was federalized *(Physical + some indexed on Ancestry/FamilySearch)*.
- **Passenger lists:** Philadelphia arrival lists on Ancestry and FamilySearch, indexed for most years. For German immigrants pre-1850, the **Strassburger-Hinke** Philadelphia German passenger lists are standard.
- **Note:** Post-1906 naturalization petitions contain extensive biographical data including birthplace, arrival date, spouse, children — extremely valuable for Eastern European research.

### Tax Records

- **Pennsylvania county tax lists:** Available for Schuylkill County from the early 1810s; earlier lists exist in Berks and Northampton for the same territory.
- **FamilySearch collection:** "Pennsylvania Tax Records, 1782–1884" — covers Schuylkill County townships; *(Indexed + images)* — an excellent substitute for missing census years, particularly the 1890 gap.
- **Later tax records (post-1884):** Held at Schuylkill County Assessment Office, Pottsville *(Physical only)*.
- **Value:** Tax lists identify real property owners annually, track economic change, and sometimes capture residents not in census records (boarders who owned property, widows retaining land).

### Occupational Records — Mining (Schuylkill County Specific)

This record type is **unique to the coal-region context** and should not be overlooked:

- **Mine employment rolls / colliery records:** Maintained by individual coal companies (Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company, Lehigh Coal and Navigation, individual operators). Record survival is uneven.
  - **Hagley Museum and Library** (Wilmington, DE): Holds Philadelphia and Reading Railroad / Coal and Iron Company archives — the most important repository for colliery records.
  - **Pennsylvania State Archives:** Some mine inspection records and state-regulated records.
- **Mine accident / coroner's records:** Schuylkill County Coroner's inquest records are held at the county courthouse *(Physical only)*; mine accident reports also in the Miners' Journal.
- **Workers' compensation:** Pennsylvania workers' compensation began 1915; records at Pennsylvania Department of Labor *(Physical only / restricted)*.
- **Union records (UMWA):** United Mine Workers of America records are partially held at **Pennsylvania State University's Paterno Library Special Collections** *(Physical)*.

### Other Record Types

- **School records:** District-level; survival inconsistent. Some at local school district offices or county historical society *(Physical only)*.
- **City/borough directories:** Pottsville, Shenandoah, Mahanoy City, and Tamaqua directories exist for various decades; useful for locating addresses and occupations between census years. Many on **Ancestry** ("U.S. City Directories") *(Indexed + images)* and **FamilySearch Catalog** *(Microfilm/Browse-only)*.
- **Fraternal organization records:** Ancient Order of Hibernians (Irish Catholic), IOOF, Masons, and mining-related fraternal orders had lodges throughout the county. Some records survive at lodges or historical societies *(Physical only)*.
- **Hospital records:** Miners' Hospital (Fountain Springs / Ashland) — one of the first miners' hospitals in the US; some records at the county historical society *(Physical only / restricted)*.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key Collections for Schuylkill County | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Federal census 1820–1940; PA death certificates 1906–1968; PA birth certificates 1906–1928; PA marriage records 1885–1950; PA probate records; PA naturalization records; PA tax records 1782–1884; church record microfilm; Civil War pension index | **Free** |
| **Ancestry** (ancestry.com) | All federal censuses; PA vital certificates; PA county marriages; city directories; Miners' Journal (partial); Philadelphia passenger lists; naturalization records; military draft cards; Fold3 military records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Civil War service records and pension files; WWI/WWII draft cards; military unit records | Subscription (some free) |
| **Newspapers.com** | Miners' Journal (partial run); other PA papers | Subscription (Ancestry bundle) |
| **Chronicling America** (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) | Selected PA newspapers pre-1924 | **Free** |
| **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery and headstone records, Schuylkill County | **Free** |
| **BillionGraves** (billiongraves.com) | Headstone photos | **Free** |
| **PA Digital Collections / PHMC** (digitalncc.libraries.psu.edu) | Selected PA historical materials | **Free** |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Schuylkill County Historical Society**, 305 N. Centre St., Pottsville, PA 17901 | Local histories, church records, newspaper runs (Miners' Journal, others), cemetery transcriptions, funeral home records, genealogical files, photographs, mining records | In-person; limited mail research; call ahead: (570) 622-7540 |
| **Schuylkill County Courthouse**, Pottsville | Deeds (Recorder of Deeds), wills/probate (Register of Wills), court records (Prothonotary), marriage licenses (Clerk of Orphans' Court) | In-person; some mail requests |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, 350 North Street, Harrisburg, PA 17120 | PA military records, warrant/patent records, naturalization records, state agency records, PA German church records | In-person; online catalog at phmc.pa.gov |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania**, 1300 Locust St., Philadelphia, PA 19107 | Large manuscript collections; genealogical society library; church records; Philadelphia-area immigration records | In-person; fee for non-members |
| **Pottsville Free Public Library** | Local newspaper microfilm; Pennsylvania Room with local history materials | In-person |
| **Diocese of Allentown Archives** | Catholic parish sacramental registers for Schuylkill County parishes | Written/email request; access by appointment |
| **Hagley Museum and Library**, Wilmington, DE | Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company records; colliery employment records | In-person; advance appointment recommended |
| **NARA Philadelphia** (National Archives Mid-Atlantic), 900 Market St., Philadelphia, PA 19107 | Federal court records (naturalizations post-1906); federal agency records | In-person; some records on Ancestry/FamilySearch |
| **Penn State University Libraries, Special Collections**, University Park, PA | UMWA union records; coal industry archives | In-person |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records **exist** but are **not available in any major online database** — do not assume they are lost just because they don't appear in a search:

1. **County deed books post-~1870s** — Recorder of Deeds, Pottsville (physical only)
2. **County tax assessment records post-1884** — County Assessment Office (physical only)
3. **Church records for most Eastern European immigrant parishes** (Slovak, Polish, Ukrainian, Lithuanian) — held at individual parishes or diocesan archives; not microfilmed by FamilySearch
4. **Mining company employment rolls** — Hagley Museum and company archives (physical only)
5. **Mine accident / coroner's inquests** — County Courthouse (physical only)
6. **Most funeral home records** — Individual funeral homes or county historical society (physical only)
7. **Local fraternal organization records** (AOH, IOOF, UMW local lodges) — lodges or historical society (physical only)
8. **School enrollment records** — Local district offices (physical only)
9. **Borough and township minute books** — Individual municipality offices (physical only)
10. **Workers' compensation records post-1915** — PA Department of Labor (physical; access may be restricted)

---

## Known Record Losses

Schuylkill County's courthouse records are **relatively well preserved** — no catastrophic courthouse fire is documented for the county.

| Loss | Details | Substitute Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Federal census, 1890** | Nationally destroyed in 1921 Washington DC fire; only ~6,000 scattered fragments survive nationally | 1880 + 1900 census; PA tax records 1882–1884; Miners' Journal; church membership rolls; city directories |
| **Pre-1906 civil vital records** | Pennsylvania had no civil vital registration before 1906 | Church registers; cemetery records; obituaries in Miners' Journal; probate records; family bibles |
| **Pre-1885 civil marriage records** | County did not record marriages before 1885 | Church registers; newspaper marriage notices; probate/estate records naming spouses |
| **Scattered mining company records** | Many 19th-century colliery records were discarded by companies as unprofitable to keep | Miners' Journal accident/employment coverage; coroner's inquests; union records |

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the Miners' Journal.** For any Schuylkill County ancestor from 1825–1950, the *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville) is often more useful than any other single source. It carried birth, marriage, and death notices; legal notices (probate, sheriff's sales, naturalizations); accident reports; labor news; and community news. Get it on microfilm at Pottsville Free Public Library or partially on Newspapers.com.

2. **Bridge the 1890 gap with tax records.** FamilySearch's "Pennsylvania Tax Records, 1782–1884" carries you to the edge; then use church records, the Miners' Journal, and city directories to cross to the 1900 census.

3. **For pre-1811 ancestors**, search **Berks County** records (Reading) for families in the southern townships (Orwigsburg, Auburn, Pine Grove areas) and **Northampton County** records (Easton) for the northern townships (Tamaqua, Mahanoy area).

4. **Spelling variation is extreme.** Eastern European names were anglicized, phonetically transcribed, and spelled inconsistently across records. A Slovak "Chovanec" may appear as "Chovanetz," "Chovenec," or even "Evans." Search FamilySearch Soundex and use wildcard searches on Ancestry.

5. **Identify the ethnic parish.** Immigrants worshipped in their own national parishes. If you know an ancestor was Slovak Catholic, Greek Catholic, Polish Catholic, or Ukrainian, identify the specific parish they would have used — don't search only Irish Catholic registers.

6. **Check the 48th Pennsylvania Infantry records** for Civil War ancestors. This regiment (and several others recruited in the county) has excellent documentation, published regimental histories, and pension files on Fold3.

7. **Naturalization records before 1906** were filed in any Pennsylvania court — not just federal court. Check the Schuylkill County Court of Common Pleas naturalization dockets, which FamilySearch has partially imaged.

8. **The FamilySearch Catalog** (catalog.familysearch.org) is the definitive guide to what FamilySearch has microfilmed for this county. Search by *Place: Pennsylvania, Schuylkill* to see the full inventory of filmed records by category.

9. **Contact the Schuylkill County Historical Society** early. Their library holds unique finding aids, funeral home records, and surname files that are not replicated anywhere online.

10. **Probate records fill vital record gaps.** An estate inventory or will from the 1820s–1890s often names a widow, all children (with married names), and sometimes ages or residences — reconstructing a whole family when no birth certificates exist.

------

## What to Do Next

This guide answers *what exists and where*. Your logical next steps:

- **If you want a prioritized search plan** → use the `research-plan` skill, which will sequence these record types around your specific research questions.
- **If you want historical context** on the coal region's migration patterns, the Molly Maguires era, or Eastern European immigration → use the `historical-context` skill.
- **If you want to search records right now** → use the `search-records` or `search-external-sites` skill with a specific ancestor's name and years.

If you let me know the **time period** and the **surnames or ethnic background** you're researching, I can make all of this much more targeted.

output.text_response:
Here's a well-structured Python function for parsing CSV files:

```python
import csv
from pathlib import Path


def parse_csv(file_path: str, delimiter: str = ",", has_header: bool = True) -> list[dict | list]:
    """
    Parse a CSV file and return its contents.

    Args:
        file_path:   Path to the CSV file.
        delimiter:   Column delimiter (default: ',').
        has_header:  If True, returns a list of dicts (column name → value).
                     If False, returns a list of lists (raw rows).

    Returns:
        List of dicts (with header) or list of lists (without header).

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If the file does not exist.
        ValueError:        If the file is empty.
    """
    path = Path(file_path)

    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"CSV file not found: {file_path}")

    rows = []

    with path.open(newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        if has_header:
            reader = csv.DictReader(f, delimiter=delimiter)
            for row in reader:
                rows.append(dict(row))
        else:
            reader = csv.reader(f, delimiter=delimiter)
            for row in reader:
                rows.append(row)

    if not rows:
        raise ValueError(f"CSV file is empty: {file_path}")

    return rows
```

---

### Usage Examples

**With a header row** (most common):
```python
# data.csv:
# name,age,city
# Alice,30,New York
# Bob,25,Los Angeles

rows = parse_csv("data.csv")
# [{"name": "Alice", "age": "30", "city": "New York"}, ...]

print(rows[0]["name"])  # Alice
```

**Without a header row:**
```python
rows = parse_csv("data.csv", has_header=False)
# [["name", "age", "city"], ["Alice", "30", "New York"], ...]
```

**Custom delimiter (e.g. TSV):**
```python
rows = parse_csv("data.tsv", delimiter="\t")
```

---

### Optional Enhancement — Type Coercion

If you want numbers converted automatically:

```python
def coerce_types(row: dict) -> dict:
    """Convert numeric strings to int or float."""
    result = {}
    for key, value in row.items():
        try:
            result[key] = int(value)
        except ValueError:
            try:
                result[key] = float(value)
            except ValueError:
                result[key] = value
    return result

# Use it after parsing:
rows = [coerce_types(row) for row in parse_csv("data.csv")]
# {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "New York"}  ← age is now int
```

---

### Key design decisions:
| Choice | Reason |
|---|---|
| `csv.DictReader` | Automatically maps headers to values — much easier to work with |
| `Path` from `pathlib` | Safer, cross-platform file handling |
| `newline=""` | Required by the `csv` module to handle line endings correctly |
| `encoding="utf-8"` | Sensible modern default; change to `"latin-1"` for older files |

Let me know if you'd like to add error handling for malformed rows, streaming for large files, or writing CSV output as well!

Now I'll fill in the template and save the file.The file **`italian-birth-records.md`** has been saved to your working folder. Here's a summary of what the FamilySearch Wiki returned:

---

### 🇮🇹 Finding Italian Birth Records

**Civil Registration (Stato Civile) — 1809/1866 onward**
Civil registration began in most of Italy under Napoleonic rule in 1809, lapsed after 1815, and resumed nationwide in **1866** after unification. **Civil birth records (atti di nascita)** are the most useful source for post-1866 Italian genealogy and include:
- Child's name, date, and place of birth
- Parents' names, ages, occupations, and residence

**Parish Registers (Registri Parrocchiali) — pre-1866 & parallel**
For ancestors born before civil registration — or as a supplement — Catholic **baptismal records (atti di battesimo)** are the go-to source. They record baptism date, child's name, parents' names, and godparents. Many Italian dioceses have registers dating back to the **late 1500s** (post-Council of Trent).

**Accessing the Records**
- Many records are digitized and findable via the **FamilySearch Catalog**, searchable by *comune* (town).
- Records **less than 100 years old** are restricted under Italian privacy law — you'll need to contact the local **Ufficio dello Stato Civile** directly.

# Albert Einstein

Albert Einstein (14 March 1879 – 18 April 1955) was a German-born theoretical physicist who is widely held to be one of the greatest and most influential scientists of all time. Best known for developing the theory of relativity, Einstein also made important contributions to quantum mechanics. He emigrated from Germany to the United States in 1933 and became a U.S. citizen in 1940.

---
[Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein)
